title: ML & Agentic AI Applications in EDA
blurb: ★ Overview only · Electronic Design Automation is where all the course's ML ideas come together. This chapter surveys how supervised learning is integrated across the design flow as fast surrogate predictors and decision-makers, and how the emerging wave of agentic AI (LLM + tools + memory in a loop) is reshaping chip design.

knowledge_points.title: Supervised Learning in EDA
knowledge_points.recap: EDA tools assist in designing/manufacturing electronic systems (design, verification, synthesis, physical design). As chips reach billions of transistors, classical EDA algorithms with polynomial complexity strain on **scale, time-to-market, productivity and cost**. **Supervised learning** learns a mapping from inputs (design parameters) to outputs (performance metrics). Its pros are **predictive power, speed** (a trained model gives fast predictions regardless of circuit size, $O(n^2)\to O(1)$) and **transferability**; its cons are **data acquisition cost, hyperparameter tuning, and overfitting / out-of-distribution** errors. SL is applied across the flow (RTL, logic synthesis, physical design, manufacturing, testing) for performance prediction, black-box optimization / design-space exploration, and design automation.

questions.q: Explain how supervised learning is used as a surrogate model / black-box optimizer in EDA and why this is attractive.
questions.answer: A **supervised learning model** is trained on data from expensive EDA simulators to **predict performance metrics (QoR, PPA, timing, power, congestion, reliability)** directly from design parameters, acting as a fast **surrogate** that can **replace computationally expensive simulators** during **design-space exploration / black-box optimization**. It is attractive because although **training takes significant time (a one-time cost)**, the trained model then gives **fast predictions regardless of circuit size** ($O(n^2)$ or worse collapsing toward $O(1)$ inference) and offers **transferability** — reusing models across technology nodes, design stages and circuits. This lets designers evaluate or optimize many candidate configurations far faster than running full simulations, and can even directly optimize/balance PPA trade-offs (e.g. learning-driven gate sizing).
questions.extend: Examples: CNN thermal-map prediction, GNN timing prediction (~80x faster than OpenSTA), crosstalk delay prediction, and cross-stage congestion/DRC/IR-drop prediction on datasets like CircuitNet.

questions.q: Which of the following are genuine supervised-learning applications across the EDA design flow?
questions.answer: Hardware Trojan detection at RTL, logic-synthesis architecture/optimizer selection, lithography hotspot and routability prediction, and manufacturing yield/test prediction.

options.text: RTL: hardware Trojan detection and RTL design-quality assessment (e.g. XGBoost/Random Forest).
Logic synthesis: selecting adder/multiplier architectures or which optimizer to apply (LSOracle).
Physical design: lithography hotspot detection and routability/congestion prediction.
Manufacturing: predicting final memory-test results from die electrical-test data to improve yield.

questions.q: What are the main current problems and limitations of applying supervised learning in EDA?
questions.answer: Key problems include: **training data** — scarce high-volume, high-quality public data (especially at physical-design level such as lithography mask images), long-tailed distributions, and data-security attacks; **overfitting / out-of-distribution** — models overestimate on unseen inputs outside the training distribution; **algorithm selection** — different EDA tasks need different architectures, requiring domain expertise; and **explainability & reliability** — it is hard to trust and understand ML decisions. Also, **data acquisition is expensive** (IP-sensitive, time-consuming) and models need careful hyperparameter tuning. Future directions address these via public benchmarks, transfer learning / foundation-model adaptation, data augmentation and synthetic data.
questions.extend: There are also security/reliability concerns — e.g. backdoor/poisoning attacks on DNN lithographic hotspot detectors.

knowledge_points.title: Agentic AI in EDA
knowledge_points.recap: **Agentic AI** = software entities that **perceive, reason, act via tools, and learn from feedback**, usually built on **LLMs + tools** (simulators, synthesizers, P&R, linters) operating in a loop: **plan -> call tools -> observe -> refine**. Core components are a **Brain (LLM)** for reasoning/planning, **Tools** to act on the world, and **Memory** (short- and long-term). EDA is evolving from manual (L0-L1) through the ML era (L2-L3, optimizing single steps) to the **Agentic AI era (L4/L5)** that coordinates multiple tools and adapts to design feedback. A hybrid future pairs **LLMs as orchestrators/planners with small language models (SLMs) as cheap executors**, with heavy emphasis on monitoring/verifying agent behavior.

questions.q: Define agentic AI and describe its three main components and its operating loop in the context of chip design.
questions.answer: **Agentic AI** refers to software entities that can **perceive, reason, act in tools, and learn from feedback**, typically built on **LLMs plus tools** (EDA simulators, synthesizers, place-and-route, linters). Its three main components are: the **Brain (LLM)** which handles reasoning, planning and language generation (defines goals and detailed plans); **Tools**, how the agent interacts with the outside world (data tools, action tools, orchestration tools); and **Memory**, giving it short-term (conversation history) and long-term context so it improves over iterations. It operates in a loop: **plan -> call tools -> observe results -> refine actions**. In EDA this lets an agent interpret a spec, generate RTL, run tools, read the results, and iterate in a closed feedback loop across design stages.
questions.extend: What agentic AI adds over plain ML/GenAI: multi-step planning, tool orchestration, knowledge integration, and self-reflection loops.

questions.q: How does the lecture characterize the evolution of ML toward agentic EDA?
questions.answer: Manual era (L0-L1) -> ML era (L2-L3, optimizing a single step like placement) -> Agentic era (L4) coordinating multiple tools and adapting to feedback.

options.text: L0-L1 manual era with fixed logic; L2-L3 ML era optimizing a single step; L4 agentic era coordinating multiple tools and adapting to design goals.
Traditional ML in EDA acted mainly as a task-specific helper predicting metrics within fixed, closed flows.
Agentic AI removes the need for any EDA tools by generating GDSII directly from a single LLM prompt.
Autonomy levels only apply to analog design and never to digital flows.

questions.q: Why is a hybrid 'LLM planner + SLM executor' architecture attractive for agentic EDA, and why does monitoring agent performance matter?
questions.answer: **Small language models (SLMs)** are far cheaper (10-30x inference savings in energy, latency, hardware), can be fine-tuned on new data in hours, and a specialized 3B-7B SLM can outperform trillion-parameter models in a focused domain, while keeping sensitive data on-premise. So the hybrid uses **LLMs as orchestrators for planning** and **SLMs as executors for repetitive tasks** — strategic model tiering reserves frontier LLMs for high-complexity reasoning. **Monitoring matters** because agents run long multi-step, tool-using loops: trajectory-first auditing and state-grounded scoring (using verifiable EDA outputs like logs and file states) give reproducible, tamper-proof metrics, detect **goal drift and error accumulation** early before small mistakes cascade into expensive failures, and enable closed-loop verification (Pass@k, time-to-first-success) so agents can be trusted at scale.
questions.extend: Industrial examples: Synopsys.ai (DSO.ai uses reinforcement learning to search RTL-to-GDSII permutations), Cadence JedAI, NVIDIA ChipNeMo, ChatEDA and ChipAgents.

knowledge_points.title: 🔮 SS26 Likely Exam Questions (predicted)
knowledge_points.recap: Predicted, exam-style concept questions in the professor's format. Not from a past paper; use to rehearse likely SS26 topics.

questions.q: (a) Explain how a **supervised learning model** is used as a **surrogate** in the EDA design flow and why this is attractive despite the training cost. (b) State two limitations of applying supervised learning in EDA.
questions.answer: (a) A supervised model is trained on data from expensive EDA simulators to **predict performance metrics (PPA, timing, power, congestion, reliability) directly from design parameters**, acting as a fast **surrogate** that replaces the slow simulator during **design-space exploration / black-box optimization**. Although training is a **significant one-time cost**, inference is then **fast regardless of circuit size** (collapsing from $O(n^2)$-or-worse simulation toward roughly $O(1)$ prediction) and models are **transferable** across nodes/stages, so many candidate designs can be evaluated far faster. (b) Limitations include: **scarce high-quality public training data** (and data-acquisition cost, IP sensitivity); **overfitting / out-of-distribution errors** on unseen inputs; **algorithm-selection difficulty** (different tasks need different architectures); and **poor explainability/reliability**.
questions.extend: Examples: GNN timing prediction (~80x faster than OpenSTA), CNN thermal-map prediction, congestion/DRC/IR-drop prediction (CircuitNet).

questions.q: Define **agentic AI** and describe its **three main components** and its **operating loop** in the context of chip design.
questions.answer: **Agentic AI** refers to software entities that can **perceive, reason, act through tools, and learn from feedback**, typically built on an **LLM plus tools** (simulators, synthesizers, place-and-route, linters). Its three main components are: the **Brain (LLM)** for reasoning, planning and language generation (setting goals and plans); **Tools**, the means by which it acts on the outside world (data/action/orchestration tools); and **Memory**, both short-term (conversation/context) and long-term, so it improves over iterations. It runs in a loop: **plan → call tools → observe results → refine**. In EDA an agent can interpret a spec, generate RTL, run tools, read the results, and iterate in a **closed feedback loop** across design stages.
questions.extend: Over plain ML/GenAI, agentic AI adds multi-step planning, tool orchestration, knowledge integration and self-reflection. EDA autonomy evolves L0-L1 (manual) → L2-L3 (ML optimizing single steps) → L4/L5 (agentic, coordinating tools).

questions.q: Which statements about the hybrid 'LLM planner + SLM executor' vision and monitoring of agentic EDA are correct?
questions.answer: SLMs are cheaper/specializable executors while LLMs orchestrate planning; monitoring catches goal drift/error accumulation using verifiable EDA outputs; model tiering reserves frontier LLMs for hard reasoning.

options.text: Small language models (SLMs) are much cheaper and can be fine-tuned quickly, making them good executors for repetitive tasks while LLMs orchestrate planning.
Monitoring agent trajectories with state-grounded scoring (logs, file states) detects goal drift and error accumulation before failures cascade.
Strategic model tiering reserves frontier LLMs for high-complexity reasoning and delegates routine work to cheaper models.
A single LLM prompt can directly output correct GDSII, eliminating the need for any EDA tools or verification.

questions.extend: Industrial examples: Synopsys DSO.ai (RL search of RTL-to-GDSII), Cadence JedAI, NVIDIA ChipNeMo, ChatEDA, ChipAgents.
